title: Der Teufel mit den drei goldenen Haaren
titleEn: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
part: The Devil with the Three Golden Hairs
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 29. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: KHM 29 in the Grimms’ collection, the tale of a poor woman’s son born with a caul (Glückshaut) and fated to marry the king’s daughter. Twice the king tries to destroy him — casting him into deep water as an infant, then sending him with a death-letter that robbers secretly rewrite — and twice fate wins; married at last, the youth is sent to hell to fetch three golden hairs from the Devil’s head, and on the road takes up three riddles (a dry fountain, a barren apple tree, a ferryman who cannot stop rowing) that the Devil’s grandmother draws out of him in his sleep. He returns rich and safe, while the greedy king, tricked into taking the ferryman’s pole, is left to row for ever. The text follows the 1857 seventh edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, gieng, that/thun, ward, erschrack, Noth, Rath, Thore/Thorwächter, getödtet and Fluß.

sentences.t: Es war einmal eine arme Frau, die gebar ein Söhnlein, und weil es eine Glückshaut um hatte, als es zur Welt kam, so ward ihm geweissagt es werde im vierzehnten Jahr die Tochter des Königs zur Frau haben.
sentences.l: There was once a poor woman, who bore a little son, and because it had a caul about it when it came to the world, so it was prophesied to it that it would in its fourteenth year have the king’s daughter for a wife.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a poor woman who bore a little son, and because he had a caul about him when he came into the world, it was foretold of him that in his fourteenth year he would have the king’s daughter for his wife.
sentences.n: “Glückshaut” (lit. ‘luck-skin’) is the caul — the piece of amniotic membrane a child is now and then born with; German folklore made it a sure sign of a “Glückskind,” a child of fortune, and the whole tale turns on it. “ward ... geweissagt” uses the archaic ward (preterite of werden) to form the passive ‘it was foretold.’ “es” is grammatically neuter (das Söhnlein / Kind), so ‘it’ where we mean ‘he.’

sentences.t: Es trug sich zu, daß der König bald darauf ins Dorf kam, und niemand wußte daß es der König war, und als er die Leute fragte was es Neues gäbe, so antworteten sie „es ist in diesen Tagen ein Kind mit einer Glückshaut geboren: was so einer unternimmt, das schlägt ihm zum Glück aus.
sentences.l: It came about that the king soon after came into the village, and no one knew that it was the king, and when he asked the people what was new, they answered, “in these days a child has been born with a caul: whatever such a one undertakes, that turns out to his good fortune.
sentences.i: It came to pass that the king soon afterward came into the village, and no one knew that it was the king; and when he asked the people what news there was, they answered, “these days a child has been born with a caul: whatever such a one sets his hand to turns out to his good fortune.
sentences.n: “trug sich zu” is the solemn fairy-tale phrase sich zutragen (‘to come about, come to pass’), which you will meet again and again in Grimm. “schlägt ... aus” is the separable verb ausschlagen; with “zum Glück” it means ‘to turn out fortunately.’

sentences.t: Es ist ihm auch voraus gesagt, in seinem vierzehnten Jahre solle er die Tochter des Königs zur Frau haben.“
sentences.l: It is also foretold to him that in his fourteenth year he shall have the king’s daughter for a wife.”
sentences.i: And it has been foretold him too that in his fourteenth year he shall have the king’s daughter for his wife.”
sentences.n: “voraus gesagt” = foretold (voraussagen, ‘to say in advance’). “solle” is subjunctive I — the subjunctive of reported speech, reporting the prophecy at second hand; it turns up constantly in these tales. “Jahre” carries the old dative -e ending.

sentences.t: Der König, der ein böses Herz hatte und über die Weissagung sich ärgerte, gieng zu den Eltern, that ganz freundlich und sagte „ihr armen Leute, überlaßt mir euer Kind, ich will es versorgen.“
sentences.l: The king, who had an evil heart and was vexed over the prophecy, went to the parents, acted quite friendly and said, “you poor people, leave your child to me, I will provide for it.”
sentences.i: The king, who had an evil heart and was angered by the prophecy, went to the parents, made himself very friendly, and said, “you poor folk, hand your child over to me; I will provide for it.”
sentences.n: “gieng” and “that” are the 1857 spellings of ging (‘went’) and tat (‘did’); this edition keeps the -ie- of gehen and the old th- of thun (modern tun). “überlaßt mir” is the imperative of überlassen (‘to leave, hand over’) with the dative mir.

sentences.t: Anfangs weigerten sie sich, da aber der fremde Mann schweres Gold dafür bot, und sie dachten „es ist ein Glückskind, es muß doch zu seinem Besten ausschlagen,“ so willigten sie endlich ein und gaben ihm das Kind.
sentences.l: At first they refused; but since the strange man offered heavy gold for it, and they thought, “it is a luck-child, it must surely turn out to its best,” they finally consented and gave him the child.
sentences.i: At first they refused; but since the stranger offered heavy gold for it, and they thought, “it is a child of fortune, surely it must turn out for the best,” they at last consented and gave him the child.
sentences.n: “weigerten sich” (sich weigern, ‘to refuse’) and “willigten ... ein” (einwilligen, ‘to consent,’ separable ein-) frame the change of heart. “schweres Gold” is literally ‘heavy gold,’ i.e. a great weight of it.

sentences.t: Der König legte es in eine Schachtel und ritt damit weiter bis er zu einem tiefen Wasser kam: da warf er die Schachtel hinein und dachte „von dem unerwarteten Freier habe ich meine Tochter geholfen.“
sentences.l: The king laid it in a box and rode on with it until he came to a deep water: there he threw the box in and thought, “from the unexpected suitor I have delivered my daughter.”
sentences.i: The king laid it in a box and rode on with it until he came to a deep water; there he threw the box in and thought, “now I have rid my daughter of the unwelcome suitor.”
sentences.n: “geholfen” (past participle of helfen) is used loosely here with an accusative object — ‘I have helped/delivered my daughter [away] from the suitor’; helfen normally takes the dative, and the phrasing is an old colloquial turn. “hinein” = in(to), away from the speaker.

sentences.t: Die Schachtel aber gieng nicht unter, sondern schwamm wie ein Schiffchen, und es drang auch kein Tröpfchen Wasser hinein.
sentences.l: But the box did not go under, rather it swam like a little ship, and not even a little drop of water pressed in.
sentences.i: But the box did not sink; instead it floated like a little ship, and not so much as a drop of water got in.
sentences.n: “gieng ... unter” is the separable verb untergehen (‘to sink, go down’). “Schiffchen” and “Tröpfchen” are diminutives (little ship, little drop) — the -chen suffix, everywhere in these tales.

sentences.t: So schwamm sie bis zwei Meilen von des Königs Hauptstadt, wo eine Mühle war, an dessen Wehr sie hängen blieb.
sentences.l: So it swam until two miles from the king’s capital, where a mill was, on whose weir it stayed hanging.
sentences.i: So it floated to within two miles of the king’s capital, where there was a mill, and on its weir it caught fast.
sentences.n: “hängen blieb” (hängen bleiben) = to catch, stick, remain hanging. A Wehr is the weir or dam that channels water to a mill-wheel.

sentences.t: Ein Mahlbursche, der glücklicherweise da stand und sie bemerkte, zog sie mit einem Haken heran und meinte große Schätze zu finden, als er sie aber aufmachte, lag ein schöner Knabe darin, der ganz frisch und munter war.
sentences.l: A mill-hand, who luckily stood there and noticed it, drew it near with a hook and supposed he would find great treasures; but when he opened it, a fair boy lay in it, who was quite fresh and lively.
sentences.i: A mill-hand who by good luck was standing there and spied it drew it in with a hook, thinking to find great treasure; but when he opened it, there lay a fair boy inside, quite fresh and lively.
sentences.n: “zog ... heran” (heranziehen, ‘to draw near’) is separable. “meinte ... zu finden” — meinen here is ‘to suppose, expect,’ not ‘to mean.’

sentences.t: Er brachte ihn zu den Müllersleuten, und weil diese keine Kinder hatten, freuten sie sich und sprachen „Gott hat es uns beschert.“
sentences.l: He brought him to the miller’s folk, and because these had no children, they rejoiced and said, “God has bestowed it on us.”
sentences.i: He brought him to the miller and his wife, and because they had no children, they rejoiced and said, “God has granted it to us.”
sentences.n: “Müllersleute” = the miller’s people (the miller and his wife). “beschert” (bescheren) is used of a gift bestowed, especially a providential one — the word still used for what one is ‘given’ at Christmas.

sentences.t: Sie pflegten den Fündling wohl, und er wuchs in allen Tugenden heran.
sentences.l: They tended the foundling well, and he grew up in all virtues.
sentences.i: They cared well for the foundling, and he grew up in every virtue.
sentences.n: “wuchs ... heran” (heranwachsen, ‘to grow up’) is separable. “Fündling” is the older spelling of Findling, a foundling.

sentences.t: Es trug sich zu, daß der König einmal bei einem Gewitter in die Mühle trat und die Müllersleute fragte ob der große Junge ihr Sohn wäre.
sentences.l: It came about that the king once, during a thunderstorm, stepped into the mill and asked the miller’s folk whether the big boy was their son.
sentences.i: It came to pass that the king once, during a thunderstorm, stepped into the mill and asked the millers whether the big lad was their son.
sentences.n: “wäre” is subjunctive II in the indirect question ‘whether he was their son.’

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antworteten sie, „es ist ein Fündling, er ist vor vierzehn Jahren in einer Schachtel ans Wehr geschwommen, und der Mahlbursche hat ihn aus dem Wasser gezogen.“
sentences.l: “No,” they answered, “it is a foundling; fourteen years ago he swam to the weir in a box, and the mill-hand pulled him out of the water.”
sentences.i: “No,” they answered, “he is a foundling; fourteen years ago he floated to the weir in a box, and the mill-hand drew him out of the water.”
sentences.n: “geschwommen” is the past participle of schwimmen (‘to swim, float’); with sein it makes the perfect of motion, ‘he came floating.’

sentences.t: Da merkte der König daß es niemand anders, als das Glückskind war, das er ins Wasser geworfen hatte, und sprach „ihr guten Leute, könnte der Junge nicht einen Brief an die Frau Königin bringen, ich will ihm zwei Goldstücke zum Lohn geben?“
sentences.l: Then the king noticed that it was none other than the luck-child that he had thrown into the water, and said, “you good people, could the boy not carry a letter to the lady queen? I will give him two gold pieces for a reward.”
sentences.i: Then the king perceived that this was none other than the child of fortune he had thrown into the water, and said, “good people, could the boy not carry a letter to the queen? I will give him two gold pieces as a reward.”
sentences.n: “niemand anders als” = none other than. This is the second murder attempt setting up: the letter the king means to send is a death-warrant.

sentences.t: „Wie der Herr König gebietet,“ antworteten die Leute, und hießen den Jungen sich bereit halten.
sentences.l: “As the lord king commands,” answered the people, and bade the boy hold himself ready.
sentences.i: “As my lord the king commands,” answered the folk, and bade the boy make himself ready.
sentences.n: “hießen ... halten” — heißen here means ‘to bid, tell (someone) to do’ (not ‘to be called’); it takes a bare infinitive. “sich bereit halten” = to hold oneself ready.

sentences.t: Da schrieb der König einen Brief an die Königin, worin stand „sobald der Knabe mit diesem Schreiben angelangt ist, soll er getödtet und begraben werden, und das alles soll geschehen sein ehe ich zurückkomme.“
sentences.l: Then the king wrote a letter to the queen, in which stood, “as soon as the boy has arrived with this writing, he shall be killed and buried, and all this shall be done before I come back.”
sentences.i: Then the king wrote a letter to the queen, in which stood: “as soon as the boy arrives with this writing, he shall be killed and buried, and all this shall be done before I return.”
sentences.n: “worin stand” = ‘in which stood,’ the idiom for what a letter ‘says.’ “Schreiben” (a noun from schreiben) is a formal word for a written missive. “getödtet” is the 1857 spelling of getötet (past participle of töten, ‘to kill’).

sentences.t: Der Knabe machte sich mit diesem Briefe auf den Weg, verirrte sich aber und kam Abends in einen großen Wald.
sentences.l: The boy set off with this letter, but lost his way and came in the evening into a great forest.
sentences.i: The boy set out with this letter, but lost his way and came at evening into a great forest.
sentences.n: “machte sich ... auf den Weg” = set off, took to the road. “verirrte sich” (sich verirren, ‘to lose one’s way’) — a reflexive verb; the boy’s straying is what saves his life. “Briefe” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: In der Dunkelheit sah er ein kleines Licht, gieng darauf zu und gelangte zu einem Häuschen.
sentences.l: In the darkness he saw a little light, went toward it and reached a little house.
sentences.i: In the darkness he saw a small light, went toward it, and came to a little house.
sentences.n: “gieng ... zu” (zugehen auf, ‘to go toward’) is separable. “gelangte zu” = arrived at, reached.

sentences.t: Als er hinein trat, saß eine alte Frau beim Feuer ganz allein.
sentences.l: When he stepped in, an old woman sat by the fire quite alone.
sentences.i: When he stepped inside, an old woman was sitting by the fire all alone.
sentences.n: “hinein trat” (hineintreten, ‘to step in’) is separable.

sentences.t: Sie erschrack als sie den Knaben erblickte und sprach „wo kommst du her und wo willst du hin?“
sentences.l: She took fright when she caught sight of the boy and said, “where do you come from and where do you want to go?”
sentences.i: She started when she caught sight of the boy and said, “where do you come from, and where are you bound?”
sentences.n: “erschrack” is the 1857/archaic preterite of erschrecken (‘to take fright’); modern spelling is erschrak. “wo ... her” and “wo ... hin” split woher (‘whence’) and wohin (‘whither’), the parts thrown to the end — very common in speech.

sentences.t: „Ich komme von der Mühle,“ antwortete er, „und will zur Frau Königin, der ich einen Brief bringen soll: weil ich mich aber in dem Walde verirrt habe, so wollte ich hier gerne übernachten.“
sentences.l: “I come from the mill,” he answered, “and want to the lady queen, to whom I am to bring a letter: but because I have lost my way in the forest, I would gladly spend the night here.”
sentences.i: “I come from the mill,” he answered, “and I am bound for the queen, to whom I must bring a letter; but because I have lost my way in the forest, I should be glad to stay the night here.”
sentences.n: “will zur Frau Königin” — the verb of motion is dropped after the modal wollen (‘I want [to go] to the queen’). “der ich ... bringen soll” — der is the dative relative pronoun, ‘to whom.’

sentences.t: „Du armer Junge,“ sprach die Frau, „du bist in ein Räuberhaus gerathen, und wenn sie heim kommen, so bringen sie dich um.“
sentences.l: “You poor boy,” said the woman, “you have got into a robbers’ house, and when they come home, they will do you in.”
sentences.i: “You poor lad,” said the woman, “you have stumbled into a robbers’ house, and when they come home they will kill you.”
sentences.n: “gerathen” is the 1857 spelling of geraten (‘to get into, land in’ — usually unluckily). “bringen ... um” (umbringen, ‘to kill, do away with’) is separable.

sentences.t: „Mag kommen wer will,“ sagte der Junge, „ich fürchte mich nicht: ich bin aber so müde, daß ich nicht weiter kann,“ streckte sich auf eine Bank, und schlief ein.
sentences.l: “May come who will,” said the boy, “I am not afraid: but I am so tired that I can go no further,” stretched himself out on a bench, and fell asleep.
sentences.i: “Let come who may,” said the boy, “I am not afraid; but I am so weary that I can go no further,” — and he stretched out on a bench and fell asleep.
sentences.n: “Mag kommen wer will” is a set defiant phrase, ‘let whoever will come.’ “fürchte mich” (sich fürchten, ‘to be afraid’) is reflexive. “schlief ein” (einschlafen, ‘to fall asleep’) is separable.

sentences.t: Bald hernach kamen die Räuber und fragten zornig was da für ein fremder Knabe läge.
sentences.l: Soon thereafter the robbers came and asked angrily what sort of strange boy lay there.
sentences.i: Soon after, the robbers came and asked angrily what strange boy was lying there.
sentences.n: “was für ein” = ‘what sort of.’ “läge” is subjunctive II of liegen in the indirect question.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sagte die Alte, „es ist ein unschuldiges Kind, es hat sich im Walde verirrt, und ich habe ihn aus Barmherzigkeit aufgenommen: er soll einen Brief an die Frau Königin bringen.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” said the old woman, “it is an innocent child, it has lost its way in the forest, and I have taken him in out of mercy: he is to bring a letter to the lady queen.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the old woman, “he is an innocent child; he lost his way in the forest, and I have taken him in out of pity; he is to carry a letter to the queen.”
sentences.n: “die Alte” = the old woman (an adjective used as a noun). “aufgenommen” (aufnehmen, ‘to take in’) is separable. Note how the gender wavers: neuter “es” (das Kind) then masculine “ihn/er” (the boy).

sentences.t: Die Räuber erbrachen den Brief und lasen ihn, und es stand darin daß der Knabe sogleich, wie er ankäme, sollte ums Leben gebracht werden.
sentences.l: The robbers broke open the letter and read it, and in it stood that the boy, as soon as he arrived, should be put to death.
sentences.i: The robbers broke open the letter and read it, and in it stood that the boy, the moment he arrived, was to be put to death.
sentences.n: “erbrachen” (erbrechen, ‘to break open’ — here the seal of a letter) is the preterite. “ums Leben bringen” = to put to death, do to death. “ankäme” is subjunctive II of ankommen (‘to arrive’).

sentences.t: Da empfanden die hartherzigen Räuber Mitleid, und der Anführer zerriß den Brief und schrieb einen andern, und es stand darin so wie der Knabe ankäme, sollte er sogleich mit der Königstochter vermählt werden.
sentences.l: Then the hard-hearted robbers felt pity, and the leader tore up the letter and wrote another, and in it stood that as soon as the boy arrived, he should at once be wedded to the king’s daughter.
sentences.i: Then the hard-hearted robbers felt pity, and their leader tore up the letter and wrote another, in which stood that the moment the boy arrived, he should at once be married to the king’s daughter.
sentences.n: The switched death-letter is the tale’s hinge: even hardened robbers balk at murdering a child, and their forgery is what makes the prophecy come true. “zerriß” is the preterite of zerreißen (‘to tear apart’); the prefix zer- means to pieces.

sentences.t: Sie ließen ihn dann ruhig bis zum andern Morgen auf der Bank liegen, und als er aufgewacht war, gaben sie ihm den Brief und zeigten ihm den rechten Weg.
sentences.l: They then let him lie quietly on the bench until the next morning, and when he had woken, they gave him the letter and showed him the right way.
sentences.i: They then let him lie quietly on the bench until the next morning, and when he had woken, they gave him the letter and pointed him the right road.
sentences.n: “andern Morgen” = the next morning (andern is the old form of anderen). “aufgewacht” (aufwachen, ‘to wake up’) is separable.

sentences.t: Die Königin aber, als sie den Brief empfangen und gelesen hatte, that wie darin stand, hieß ein prächtiges Hochzeitsfest anstellen, und die Königstochter ward mit dem Glückskind vermählt; und da der Jüngling schön und freundlich war, so lebte sie vergnügt und zufrieden mit ihm.
sentences.l: But the queen, when she had received and read the letter, did as stood in it, ordered a splendid wedding feast to be arranged, and the king’s daughter was wedded to the luck-child; and since the youth was handsome and kindly, she lived merry and content with him.
sentences.i: But the queen, when she had received and read the letter, did as it said, had a splendid wedding feast made ready, and the king’s daughter was married to the child of fortune; and since the youth was handsome and kind, she lived happy and content with him.
sentences.n: “hieß ... anstellen” — heißen again in the sense ‘to order (something done)’; anstellen here = to arrange, set going. “ward ... vermählt” uses archaic ward to form the passive ‘was wedded.’

sentences.t: Nach einiger Zeit kam der König wieder in sein Schloß und sah daß die Weissagung erfüllt und das Glückskind mit seiner Tochter vermählt war.
sentences.l: After some time the king came back into his castle and saw that the prophecy was fulfilled and the luck-child wedded to his daughter.
sentences.i: After some time the king came back to his castle and saw that the prophecy was fulfilled and the child of fortune married to his daughter.
sentences.n: “Weissagung” (‘prophecy’) is the noun from weissagen; it, like the caul, is a thread that runs through the whole tale.

sentences.t: „Wie ist das zugegangen?“ sprach er, „ich habe in meinem Brief einen ganz andern Befehl ertheilt.“
sentences.l: “How has that come about?” he said, “I gave a quite different order in my letter.”
sentences.i: “How did this come to pass?” he said. “I gave a quite different command in my letter.”
sentences.n: “zugegangen” (zugehen, ‘to happen, come about’) is the past participle. “ertheilt” is the 1857 spelling of erteilt (einen Befehl erteilen = to give an order).

sentences.t: Da reichte ihm die Königin den Brief und sagte er möchte selbst sehen was darin stände.
sentences.l: Then the queen handed him the letter and said he might see for himself what stood in it.
sentences.i: Then the queen handed him the letter and said he might see for himself what it said.
sentences.n: “möchte” and “stände” are subjunctives of reported speech (‘that he might ... what stood’).

sentences.t: Der König las den Brief und merkte wohl daß er mit einem andern war vertauscht worden.
sentences.l: The king read the letter and noticed well that it had been exchanged for another.
sentences.i: The king read the letter and saw plainly that it had been swapped for another.
sentences.n: “vertauscht worden” — the passive perfect of vertauschen (‘to swap, exchange’). “wohl” here is ‘well, plainly,’ not ‘probably.’

sentences.t: Er fragte den Jüngling wie es mit dem anvertrauten Briefe zugegangen wäre, warum er einen andern dafür gebracht hätte.
sentences.l: He asked the youth how it had gone with the entrusted letter, why he had brought another in its place.
sentences.i: He asked the youth what had become of the letter entrusted to him, and why he had brought another in its stead.
sentences.n: “anvertrauten” (anvertrauen, ‘to entrust’) is a past participle used as an adjective, ‘the entrusted letter.’ “zugegangen wäre ... gebracht hätte” are subjunctive II in the reported question.

sentences.t: „Ich weiß von nichts,“ antwortete er, „er muß mir in der Nacht vertauscht sein, als ich im Walde geschlafen habe.“
sentences.l: “I know of nothing,” he answered, “it must have been swapped on me in the night, when I slept in the forest.”
sentences.i: “I know nothing of it,” he answered; “it must have been exchanged on me in the night, while I slept in the forest.”
sentences.n: “mir ... vertauscht” — the dative mir is a dative of interest, ‘swapped on me / to my cost.’

sentences.t: Voll Zorn sprach der König „so leicht soll es dir nicht werden, wer meine Tochter haben will, der muß mir aus der Hölle drei goldene Haare von dem Haupte des Teufels holen; bringst du mir was ich verlange, so sollst du meine Tochter behalten.“
sentences.l: Full of wrath the king said, “it shall not become so easy for you; whoever wants to have my daughter, he must fetch me three golden hairs from the head of the Devil out of hell; if you bring me what I demand, then you shall keep my daughter.”
sentences.i: Full of wrath the king said, “it shall not come so easily to you: whoever would have my daughter must fetch me three golden hairs from the Devil’s head out of hell; bring me what I ask, and you shall keep my daughter.”
sentences.n: Here the king sets the impossible errand — the golden-hairs quest that gives the tale its name. “Haupte” is the elevated word for ‘head’ (Haupt), with old dative -e; the everyday word Kopf comes later in hell. “bringst du ... so ...” — a conditional made by putting the verb first, ‘if you bring ..., then ...’

sentences.t: Damit hoffte der König ihn auf immer los zu werden.
sentences.l: With this the king hoped to be rid of him for ever.
sentences.i: By this the king hoped to be rid of him for good.
sentences.n: “jemanden los werden” = to get rid of someone. “auf immer” = for ever.

sentences.t: Das Glückskind aber antwortete „die goldenen Haare will ich wohl holen, ich fürchte mich vor dem Teufel nicht.“
sentences.l: But the luck-child answered, “the golden hairs I will indeed fetch; I am not afraid of the Devil.”
sentences.i: But the child of fortune answered, “the golden hairs I will fetch readily enough; I am not afraid of the Devil.”
sentences.n: “wohl” here is emphatic, ‘indeed, readily.’ “fürchte mich vor” (sich fürchten vor + dative) = to be afraid of.

sentences.t: Darauf nahm er Abschied und begann seine Wanderschaft.
sentences.l: Thereupon he took leave and began his wandering.
sentences.i: With that he took his leave and set out on his journey.
sentences.n: “Abschied nehmen” = to take one’s leave. “Wanderschaft” is the journeying itself — the wandering years.

sentences.t: Der Weg führte ihn zu einer großen Stadt, wo ihn der Wächter an dem Thore ausfragte was für ein Gewerbe er verstände und was er wüßte.
sentences.l: The way led him to a great city, where the watchman at the gate questioned him what trade he understood and what he knew.
sentences.i: The road led him to a great city, where the watchman at the gate questioned him as to what trade he understood and what he knew.
sentences.n: Now come the three questions put to him on the road, which he promises to answer on his return. “Thore” is the 1857 spelling of Tore (‘gate’), with old th-. “ausfragte” (ausfragen, ‘to question, interrogate’) is separable. “verstände ... wüßte” are subjunctive II in the reported question.

sentences.t: „Ich weiß alles“ antwortete das Glückskind.
sentences.l: “I know everything,” answered the luck-child.
sentences.i: “I know everything,” answered the child of fortune.

sentences.t: „So kannst du uns einen Gefallen thun,“ sagte der Wächter, „wenn du uns sagst warum unser Marktbrunnen, aus dem sonst Wein quoll, trocken geworden ist, und nicht einmal mehr Wasser gibt.“
sentences.l: “Then you can do us a favor,” said the watchman, “if you tell us why our market fountain, from which wine used to well up, has become dry, and no longer even gives water.”
sentences.i: “Then you can do us a favor,” said the watchman, “if you tell us why our market fountain, that once ran with wine, has gone dry and no longer even gives water.”
sentences.n: First riddle: the dry fountain. “Gefallen thun” = to do a favor (thun is the old spelling of tun). “sonst” here means ‘formerly, at other times.’ “quoll” is the preterite of quellen (‘to well up, gush’).

sentences.t: „Das sollt ihr erfahren,“ antwortete er, „wartet nur bis ich wiederkomme.“
sentences.l: “That you shall learn,” he answered, “just wait until I come back.”
sentences.i: “That you shall learn,” he answered; “only wait until I come again.”
sentences.n: “erfahren” here = to learn, find out. “wiederkomme” (wiederkommen, ‘to come back’) is separable, held together in the wenn-less clause.

sentences.t: Da gieng er weiter und kam vor eine andere Stadt, da fragte der Thorwächter wiederum was für ein Gewerb er verstünde und was er wüßte.
sentences.l: Then he went on and came before another city; there the gate-keeper asked again what trade he understood and what he knew.
sentences.i: Then he went on and came to another city, where the gate-keeper again asked what trade he understood and what he knew.
sentences.n: “Gewerb” is an older short form of Gewerbe (‘trade, craft’). “verstünde” is a variant subjunctive II of verstehen (alongside verstände above).

sentences.t: „Ich weiß alles“ antwortete er.
sentences.l: “I know everything,” he answered.

sentences.t: „So kannst du uns einen Gefallen thun, und uns sagen warum ein Baum in unserer Stadt, der sonst goldene Äpfel trug, jetzt nicht einmal Blätter hervor treibt.“
sentences.l: “Then you can do us a favor, and tell us why a tree in our city, that once bore golden apples, now does not even put forth leaves.”
sentences.i: “Then you can do us a favor, and tell us why a tree in our city, that once bore golden apples, now does not so much as put forth leaves.”
sentences.n: Second riddle: the barren apple tree. “treibt ... hervor” (hervortreiben, ‘to put/send forth’) is separable; a tree ‘drives out’ its leaves.

sentences.t: „Das sollt ihr erfahren,“ antwortete er, „wartet nur bis ich wiederkomme.“
sentences.l: “That you shall learn,” he answered, “just wait until I come back.”
sentences.i: “That you shall learn,” he answered; “only wait until I come again.”

sentences.t: Da gieng er weiter, und kam an ein großes Wasser, über das er hinüber mußte.
sentences.l: Then he went on, and came to a great water, over which he had to cross.
sentences.i: Then he went on and came to a great water, over which he had to cross.
sentences.n: “über das ... hinüber mußte” — the verb of motion is dropped after the modal (‘had [to go] over’). hinüber = across, away from the speaker.

sentences.t: Der Fährmann fragte ihn was er für ein Gewerb verstände und was er wüßte.
sentences.l: The ferryman asked him what trade he understood and what he knew.
sentences.i: The ferryman asked him what trade he understood and what he knew.

sentences.t: „Ich weiß alles“ antwortete er.
sentences.l: “I know everything,” he answered.

sentences.t: „So kannst du mir einen Gefallen thun,“ sprach der Fährmann, „und mir sagen warum ich immer hin und her fahren muß und niemals abgelöst werde.“
sentences.l: “Then you can do me a favor,” said the ferryman, “and tell me why I must always ferry to and fro and am never relieved.”
sentences.i: “Then you can do me a favor,” said the ferryman, “and tell me why I must forever row back and forth and am never relieved.”
sentences.n: Third riddle: the ferryman who can never be relieved of his oar — the curse the greedy king will inherit at the end. “hin und her” = to and fro. “abgelöst” (ablösen, ‘to relieve, take over from’) is the word for being relieved of a duty or post.

sentences.t: „Das sollst du erfahren,“ antwortete er, „warte nur bis ich wiederkomme.“
sentences.l: “That you shall learn,” he answered, “just wait until I come back.”
sentences.i: “That you shall learn,” he answered; “only wait until I come again.”

sentences.t: Als er über das Wasser hinüber war, so fand er den Eingang zur Hölle.
sentences.l: When he was over across the water, he found the entrance to hell.
sentences.i: When he was across the water, he found the entrance to hell.

sentences.t: Es war schwarz und rußig darin, und der Teufel war nicht zu Haus, aber seine Ellermutter saß da in einem breiten Sorgenstuhl.
sentences.l: It was black and sooty in there, and the Devil was not at home, but his grandmother sat there in a broad easy-chair.
sentences.i: It was black and sooty inside, and the Devil was not at home, but his grandmother sat there in a broad old armchair.
sentences.n: “Ellermutter” is a dialect/archaic word for ‘grandmother’ (elder-mother); the Devil’s grandmother is his household’s soft heart, and it is she who does the hero’s real work. “Sorgenstuhl” (lit. ‘care-chair’) is an old word for a comfortable armchair one sinks into with one’s cares. “rußig” = sooty.

sentences.t: „Was willst du?“ sprach sie zu ihm, sah aber gar nicht so böse aus.
sentences.l: “What do you want?” she said to him, but did not look so evil at all.
sentences.i: “What do you want?” she said to him, though she did not look at all so wicked.
sentences.n: “sah ... aus” (aussehen, ‘to look, appear’) is separable. “gar nicht” = not at all.

sentences.t: „Ich wollte gerne drei goldene Haare von des Teufels Kopf,“ antwortete er, „sonst kann ich meine Frau nicht behalten.“
sentences.l: “I would gladly like three golden hairs from the Devil’s head,” he answered, “otherwise I cannot keep my wife.”
sentences.i: “I should dearly like three golden hairs from the Devil’s head,” he answered, “or else I cannot keep my wife.”
sentences.n: “des Teufels Kopf” — genitive before the noun, ‘the Devil’s head’; here the plain word Kopf, unlike the king’s lofty Haupt earlier. “sonst” here = otherwise.

sentences.t: „Das ist viel verlangt,“ sagte sie, „wenn der Teufel heim kommt und findet dich, so geht dirs an den Kragen; aber du dauerst mich, ich will sehen ob ich dir helfen kann.“
sentences.l: “That is much asked,” she said, “if the Devil comes home and finds you, then it goes at your collar; but you move me to pity, I will see whether I can help you.”
sentences.i: “That is a great deal to ask,” she said; “if the Devil comes home and finds you, your life is forfeit; but I am sorry for you — I will see whether I can help you.”
sentences.n: “es geht dir an den Kragen” is an idiom, ‘it is a matter of life and death for you’ (lit. ‘it goes at your collar/neck’). “du dauerst mich” — dauern here means ‘to move to pity’: ‘you move me to pity, I am sorry for you.’

sentences.t: Sie verwandelte ihn in eine Ameise und sprach „kriech in meine Rockfalten, da bist du sicher.“
sentences.l: She transformed him into an ant and said, “creep into the folds of my skirt, there you are safe.”
sentences.i: She turned him into an ant and said, “creep into the folds of my skirt, and there you will be safe.”
sentences.n: “verwandelte ... in” (verwandeln, ‘to transform’) takes the accusative of the new shape. “Rockfalten” are the folds of a Rock (here a skirt/gown).

sentences.t: „Ja“ antwortete er, „das ist schon gut, aber drei Dinge möcht ich gerne noch wissen, warum ein Brunnen, aus dem sonst Wein quoll, trocken geworden ist, jetzt nicht einmal mehr Wasser gibt: warum ein Baum, der sonst goldene Äpfel trug, nicht einmal mehr Laub treibt, und warum ein Fährmann immer herüber und hinüber fahren muß und nicht abgelöst wird.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” he answered, “that is well and good, but three things I would gladly still know: why a fountain, from which wine used to well up, has become dry and now does not even give water; why a tree, that once bore golden apples, no longer even puts forth foliage; and why a ferryman must always ferry over here and over there and is not relieved.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” he answered, “that is all very well, but there are three things I should still like to know: why a fountain that once ran with wine has gone dry and now gives not even water; why a tree that once bore golden apples no longer even puts forth foliage; and why a ferryman must forever row this way and that and is never relieved.”
sentences.n: He now lays all three riddles before the grandmother at once — the fountain, the tree, the ferryman — so she can draw the answers out of the Devil in his sleep. “möcht” is the contracted subjunctive möchte (‘would like’). “Laub” = foliage, leaves collectively.

sentences.t: „Das sind schwere Fragen,“ antwortete sie, „aber halte dich nur still und ruhig, und hab acht was der Teufel spricht, wann ich ihm die drei goldenen Haare ausziehe.“
sentences.l: “Those are hard questions,” she answered, “but only hold yourself still and quiet, and take heed what the Devil says when I pull out the three golden hairs for him.”
sentences.i: “Those are hard questions,” she answered, “but just keep still and quiet, and take heed of what the Devil says while I pull out the three golden hairs.”
sentences.n: “hab acht” = take heed, pay attention (acht haben). “ausziehe” (ausziehen, ‘to pull out’) is separable, here held together in the wann-clause. “Fragen” is the plural of Frage (‘question’).

sentences.t: Als der Abend einbrach, kam der Teufel nach Haus.
sentences.l: When the evening broke in, the Devil came home.
sentences.i: When evening fell, the Devil came home.
sentences.n: “einbrach” (einbrechen, of night/evening, ‘to fall, close in’) is separable.

sentences.t: Kaum war er eingetreten, so merkte er daß die Luft nicht rein war.
sentences.l: Hardly had he stepped in, when he noticed that the air was not clean.
sentences.i: He had barely stepped in when he noticed that the air was not pure.
sentences.n: “Kaum ... so ...” = ‘hardly ... when ...’ “eingetreten” (eintreten, ‘to step in’) is the past participle. ‘The air is not clean’ = something is not right — he smells a human.

sentences.t: „Ich rieche rieche Menschenfleisch,“ sagte er, „es ist hier nicht richtig.“
sentences.l: “I smell, smell human flesh,” he said, “it is not right here.”
sentences.i: “I smell, I smell man’s flesh,” he said; “something is not right here.”
sentences.n: The doubled “rieche rieche” is the ogre’s sniffing refrain (compare the English ‘fee-fi-fo-fum, I smell the blood…’). “es ist nicht richtig” = something is amiss.

sentences.t: Dann guckte er in alle Ecken, und suchte, konnte aber nichts finden.
sentences.l: Then he peered into all the corners, and searched, but could find nothing.
sentences.i: Then he peered into every corner and searched, but could find nothing.

sentences.t: Die Ellermutter schalt ihn aus, „eben ist erst gekehrt“ sprach sie, „und alles in Ordnung gebracht, nun wirfst du mirs wieder untereinander; immer hast du Menschenfleisch in der Nase!
sentences.l: The grandmother scolded him, “it has just been swept,” she said, “and everything put in order; now you throw it all about for me again; you always have human flesh in your nose!
sentences.i: The grandmother scolded him: “it has only just been swept,” she said, “and everything set to rights, and now you go throwing it all into confusion again — you always have man’s flesh on the brain!
sentences.n: “schalt ... aus” (ausschelten, ‘to scold’) is separable; schalt is the preterite of schelten. “gekehrt” = swept (kehren). “wirfst ... untereinander” = throw into confusion, jumble up. “Menschenfleisch in der Nase haben” — an idiom, ‘to have human flesh on the brain,’ i.e. to be forever imagining it.

sentences.t: Setze dich nieder und iß dein Abendbrot.“
sentences.l: Sit yourself down and eat your supper.”
sentences.i: Sit down and eat your supper.”
sentences.n: “Setze dich nieder” = sit down (sich niedersetzen, separable). “Abendbrot” is the evening meal, supper.

sentences.t: Als er gegessen und getrunken hatte, war er müde, legte der Ellermutter seinen Kopf in den Schoß und sagte sie sollte ihn ein wenig lausen.
sentences.l: When he had eaten and drunk, he was tired, laid his head in the grandmother’s lap and said she should delouse him a little.
sentences.i: When he had eaten and drunk, he was weary, laid his head in the grandmother’s lap, and said she should pick his hair over a little.
sentences.n: “der Ellermutter ... in den Schoß” — der is dative, ‘into the grandmother’s lap.’ “lausen” is literally ‘to pick lice from’ someone’s hair — an old gesture of intimacy that here lets her reach the golden hairs.

sentences.t: Es dauerte nicht lange, so schlummerte er ein, blies und schnarchte.
sentences.l: It did not last long, and he dozed off, puffed and snored.
sentences.i: It was not long before he dozed off, puffing and snoring.
sentences.n: “schlummerte ... ein” (einschlummern, ‘to doze off’) is separable. “blies” is the preterite of blasen (‘to blow’), here of heavy breathing.

sentences.t: Da faßte die Alte ein goldenes Haar, riß es aus und legte es neben sich.
sentences.l: Then the old woman grasped a golden hair, pulled it out and laid it beside her.
sentences.i: Then the old woman took hold of a golden hair, plucked it out, and laid it beside her.
sentences.n: “faßte” is the preterite of fassen (‘to grasp, take hold of’). “riß ... aus” (ausreißen, ‘to tear/pluck out’) is separable — the first of the three golden hairs.

sentences.t: „Autsch!“ schrie der Teufel, „was hast du vor?“
sentences.l: “Ouch!” cried the Devil, “what are you up to?”
sentences.i: “Ouch!” cried the Devil, “what are you about?”
sentences.n: “Autsch” = ouch. “hast ... vor” (vorhaben, ‘to intend, be up to’) is separable.

sentences.t: „Ich habe einen schweren Traum gehabt,“ antwortete die Ellermutter, „da hab ich dir in die Haare gefaßt.“
sentences.l: “I had a heavy dream,” answered the grandmother, “and so I grasped you by the hair.”
sentences.i: “I had a troubled dream,” answered the grandmother, “and clutched at your hair.”
sentences.n: The grandmother’s trick: each plucked hair is blamed on a bad dream, and each ‘dream’ is one of the hero’s three riddles, drawing the answer out of the Devil. “schwer” of a dream = heavy, troubling. “gefaßt” is the 1857 spelling of the participle of fassen.

sentences.t: „Was hat dir denn geträumt?“ fragte der Teufel.
sentences.l: “What did you dream, then?” asked the Devil.
sentences.i: “And what did you dream?” asked the Devil.
sentences.n: “Was hat dir geträumt” — träumen is used impersonally with the dative: ‘what dreamt itself to you,’ i.e. what did you dream. “denn” is a softening particle.

sentences.t: „Mir hat geträumt ein Marktbrunnen, aus dem sonst Wein quoll, sei versiegt, und es habe nicht einmal Wasser daraus quellen wollen, was ist wohl Schuld daran?“
sentences.l: “I dreamt that a market fountain, from which wine used to well up, had run dry, and that not even water would well from it; what is to blame for that?”
sentences.i: “I dreamt that a market fountain, that once ran with wine, had run dry, and that not even water would well up from it; what can be the cause?”
sentences.n: First riddle put as a dream. “versiegt” = run dry (versiegen). “sei ... habe ... wollen” are subjunctive I, marking the whole thing as a reported dream. “Schuld daran sein” = to be to blame for it.

sentences.t: „He, wenn sies wüßten!“ antwortete der Teufel, „es sitzt eine Kröte unter einem Stein im Brunnen, wenn sie die tödten, so wird der Wein schon wieder fließen.“
sentences.l: “Ha, if they only knew!” answered the Devil, “a toad sits under a stone in the fountain; if they kill it, then the wine will surely flow again.”
sentences.i: “Ha, if only they knew!” answered the Devil; “there sits a toad under a stone in the fountain — if they kill it, the wine will flow again.”
sentences.n: First answer: a toad under a stone. “wenn sies wüßten” = ‘if they [only] knew’ (sies = sie es); wüßten is the 1857 spelling of the subjunctive wüssten. “Kröte” = toad. “tödten” is the 1857 spelling of töten (‘to kill’). “schon” here softens to ‘surely, all right.’

sentences.t: Die Ellermutter lauste ihn wieder, bis er einschlief und schnarchte daß die Fenster zitterten.
sentences.l: The grandmother deloused him again, until he fell asleep and snored so that the windows trembled.
sentences.i: The grandmother picked over his hair again, until he fell asleep and snored so that the windows shook.
sentences.n: “lauste” is the preterite of lausen (see above). “daß die Fenster zitterten” = ‘so that the windows trembled’ — a result clause with daß.

sentences.t: Da riß sie ihm das zweite Haar aus.
sentences.l: Then she pulled out the second hair for him.
sentences.i: Then she plucked out his second hair.
sentences.n: “riß ... aus” again (ausreißen) — the second golden hair. The dative “ihm” is a dative of possession, ‘his hair.’

sentences.t: „Hu! was machst du?“ schrie der Teufel zornig.
sentences.l: “Hu! what are you doing?” cried the Devil angrily.
sentences.i: “Ugh! what are you doing?” cried the Devil in anger.

sentences.t: „Nimms nicht übel,“ antwortete sie, „ich habe es im Traum gethan.“
sentences.l: “Don’t take it amiss,” she answered, “I did it in my dream.”
sentences.i: “Don’t take it amiss,” she answered; “I did it in my sleep.”
sentences.n: “Nimms nicht übel” = ‘don’t take it amiss’ (nimm es nicht übel); übel here is ‘ill, amiss.’ “gethan” is the 1857 spelling of getan (past participle of thun/tun).

sentences.t: „Was hat dir wieder geträumt?“ fragte er.
sentences.l: “What did you dream this time?” he asked.
sentences.i: “And what did you dream now?” he asked.

sentences.t: „Mir hat geträumt in einem Königreiche ständ ein Obstbaum, der hätte sonst goldene Äpfel getragen und wollte jetzt nicht einmal Laub treiben.
sentences.l: “I dreamt that in a kingdom there stood a fruit tree, which once had borne golden apples and now would not even put forth foliage.
sentences.i: “I dreamt that in a kingdom there stood a fruit tree, which had once borne golden apples and now would not even put forth leaves.
sentences.n: Second riddle as a dream. “ständ” is a contracted subjunctive of stehen (‘there stood’). “hätte ... getragen ... wollte” keep the reported-dream mood. “Obstbaum” = fruit tree.

sentences.t: Was war wohl die Ursache davon?“
sentences.l: What might be the cause of that?”
sentences.i: What can have been the cause of it?”
sentences.n: “Ursache” = cause, reason. “wohl” here softens the question, ‘might, can.’

sentences.t: „He, wenn sies wüßten!“ antwortete der Teufel, „an der Wurzel nagt eine Maus, wenn sie die tödten, so wird er schon wieder goldene Äpfel tragen, nagt sie aber noch länger, so verdorrt der Baum gänzlich.
sentences.l: “Ha, if they only knew!” answered the Devil, “a mouse gnaws at the root; if they kill it, then it will surely bear golden apples again; but if it gnaws much longer, then the tree withers entirely.
sentences.i: “Ha, if only they knew!” answered the Devil; “a mouse is gnawing at the root — if they kill it, the tree will bear golden apples again; but if it gnaws much longer, the tree will wither away altogether.
sentences.n: Second answer: a mouse gnawing the root. “nagt” (nagen, ‘to gnaw’). “Wurzel” = root. “verdorrt” (verdorren) = withers, dries up; “gänzlich” = entirely.

sentences.t: Aber laß mich mit deinen Träumen in Ruhe, wenn du mich noch einmal im Schlafe störst, so kriegst du eine Ohrfeige.“
sentences.l: But leave me in peace with your dreams; if you disturb me once more in my sleep, then you get a box on the ear.”
sentences.i: But leave me in peace with your dreams; if you disturb me in my sleep once more, you shall get a box on the ear.”
sentences.n: “laß mich in Ruhe” = leave me in peace (laß is the archaic imperative of lassen). “Träumen” is the dative plural of Traum. “kriegst” is colloquial for bekommst (‘you get’). An Ohrfeige is a slap on the ear.

sentences.t: Die Ellermutter sprach ihn zu gut, und lauste ihn wieder bis er eingeschlafen war und schnarchte.
sentences.l: The grandmother spoke him over kindly, and deloused him again until he had fallen asleep and snored.
sentences.i: The grandmother soothed him with kind words and picked over his hair again until he had fallen asleep and was snoring.
sentences.n: “sprach ihn zu gut” — an old turn, ‘spoke him round with good/kind words, coaxed him.’ “eingeschlafen” (einschlafen) is the past participle.

sentences.t: Da faßte sie das dritte goldene Haar und riß es ihm aus.
sentences.l: Then she grasped the third golden hair and pulled it out for him.
sentences.i: Then she took hold of the third golden hair and plucked it out.
sentences.n: The third and last golden hair.

sentences.t: Der Teufel fuhr in die Höhe, schrie und wollte übel mit ihr wirthschaften, aber sie besänftigte ihn nochmals und sprach, „wer kann für böse Träume!“
sentences.l: The Devil started up on high, cried out and wanted to deal ill with her, but she soothed him once more and said, “who can help bad dreams!”
sentences.i: The Devil started bolt upright, cried out, and would have dealt roughly with her, but she calmed him once more and said, “who can help having bad dreams!”
sentences.n: “fuhr in die Höhe” = started up, sprang up (an idiom). “übel mit ihr wirthschaften” — ‘to deal ill with her, handle her roughly’ (wirthschaften is the 1857 spelling of wirtschaften). “wer kann für böse Träume” = ‘who is to blame for bad dreams’ (für etwas können = to be responsible for). “besänftigte” = soothed, appeased.

sentences.t: „Was hat dir denn geträumt?“ fragte er, und war doch neugierig.
sentences.l: “What did you dream, then?” he asked, and was curious after all.
sentences.i: “And what did you dream?” he asked, curious in spite of himself.

sentences.t: „Mir hat von einem Fährmann geträumt, der sich beklagte daß er immer hin und her fahren müßte, und nicht abgelöst würde.
sentences.l: “I dreamt of a ferryman, who complained that he must always ferry to and fro, and was not relieved.
sentences.i: “I dreamt of a ferryman who complained that he must forever row back and forth and was never relieved.
sentences.n: Third riddle as a dream. “beklagte” (sich beklagen, ‘to complain’). “müßte ... würde” are subjunctive II in the reported complaint.

sentences.t: Was ist wohl Schuld?“
sentences.l: What is to blame?”
sentences.i: What can be the cause?”

sentences.t: „He, der Dummbart!“ antwortete der Teufel, „wenn einer kommt und will überfahren, so muß er ihm die Stange in die Hand geben, dann muß der andere überfahren und er ist frei.“
sentences.l: “Ha, the dolt!” answered the Devil, “when someone comes and wants to be ferried across, he must give him the pole into his hand; then the other must ferry across and he is free.”
sentences.i: “Ha, the blockhead!” answered the Devil; “when someone comes and wants to be ferried across, he need only put the pole into that man’s hand — then the other has to do the ferrying, and he himself is free.”
sentences.n: Third answer, and the tale’s trap: whoever holds the ferryman’s pole is bound to the oar until he passes it on. “Dummbart” (lit. ‘dumb-beard’) = dolt, blockhead. “Stange” = the punt-pole. “überfahren” here = to ferry across / be ferried over.

sentences.t: Da die Ellermutter ihm die drei goldenen Haare ausgerissen hatte und die drei Fragen beantwortet waren, so ließ sie den alten Drachen in Ruhe, und er schlief bis der Tag anbrach.
sentences.l: Since the grandmother had pulled out the three golden hairs for him and the three questions were answered, she left the old dragon in peace, and he slept until day broke.
sentences.i: Now that the grandmother had plucked out the three golden hairs and the three questions were answered, she left the old dragon in peace, and he slept until day broke.
sentences.n: “den alten Drachen” — the Devil is called ‘the old dragon,’ a common name for the Devil. “beantwortet” (beantworten, ‘to answer’). “anbrach” (anbrechen, of day, ‘to break’) is separable.

sentences.t: Als der Teufel wieder fortgezogen war, holte die Alte die Ameise aus der Rockfalte, und gab dem Glückskind die menschliche Gestalt zurück.
sentences.l: When the Devil had gone off again, the old woman fetched the ant out of the fold of her skirt, and gave the luck-child back his human shape.
sentences.i: When the Devil had gone off again, the old woman took the ant out of the fold of her skirt, and gave the child of fortune back his human form.
sentences.n: “fortgezogen” (fortziehen, ‘to move off, depart’) is the past participle. “gab ... zurück” (zurückgeben, ‘to give back’) is separable. “Gestalt” = shape, form.

sentences.t: „Da hast du die drei goldenen Haare,“ sprach sie, „was der Teufel zu deinen drei Fragen gesagt hat, wirst du wohl gehört haben.“
sentences.l: “There you have the three golden hairs,” she said, “what the Devil said to your three questions, you will surely have heard.”
sentences.i: “There you have the three golden hairs,” she said; “and what the Devil said to your three questions, you will surely have heard.”
sentences.n: “wirst ... gehört haben” — future perfect used for a confident supposition, ‘you will (surely) have heard.’

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete er, „ich habe es gehört und wills wohl behalten.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” he answered, “I have heard it and will surely keep it.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” he answered, “I have heard it, and I shall remember it well.”
sentences.n: “wills” = will es (‘will [keep] it’). “behalten” here = to keep, retain (in memory).

sentences.t: „So ist dir geholfen,“ sagte sie, „und nun kannst du deiner Wege ziehen.“
sentences.l: “So you are helped,” she said, “and now you can go your ways.”
sentences.i: “Then you are helped,” she said, “and now you can go on your way.”
sentences.n: “dir ist geholfen” — an impersonal passive with the dative, ‘you are helped.’ “deiner Wege ziehen” = to go one’s way (an old genitive idiom).

sentences.t: Er bedankte sich bei der Alten für die Hilfe in der Noth, verließ die Hölle, und war vergnügt daß ihm alles so wohl geglückt war.
sentences.l: He thanked the old woman for the help in his need, left hell, and was merry that everything had succeeded for him so well.
sentences.i: He thanked the old woman for her help in his need, left hell, and was glad that all had gone so well for him.
sentences.n: “bedankte sich bei” (sich bedanken bei, ‘to thank’) takes bei + person. “Noth” is the 1857 spelling of Not (‘need, distress’). “geglückt” (glücken, ‘to succeed’) is impersonal with the dative, ‘it succeeded for him.’

sentences.t: Als er zu dem Fährmann kam, sollte er ihm die versprochene Antwort geben.
sentences.l: When he came to the ferryman, he was to give him the promised answer.
sentences.i: When he came to the ferryman, he was to give him the answer he had promised.
sentences.n: Now he pays back the three answers in reverse order — ferryman, tree, fountain. “versprochene” (versprechen, ‘to promise’) is a participle used as an adjective.

sentences.t: „Fahr mich erst hinüber,“ sprach das Glückskind, „so will ich dir sagen wie du erlöst wirst,“ und als er auf dem jenseitigen Ufer angelangt war, gab er ihm des Teufels Rath, „wenn wieder einer kommt, und will übergefahren sein, so gib ihm nur die Stange in die Hand.“
sentences.l: “Ferry me over first,” said the luck-child, “then I will tell you how you will be released,” and when he had arrived on the far bank, he gave him the Devil’s counsel, “when someone comes again and wants to be ferried over, just give him the pole into his hand.”
sentences.i: “Ferry me over first,” said the child of fortune, “and then I will tell you how you may be released,” and when he had reached the far bank, he gave him the Devil’s counsel: “when another comes and wants to be ferried over, only put the pole into his hand.”
sentences.n: He wisely takes his own passage first, then hands over the secret. “Fahr” is the imperative of fahren (here ‘to ferry’). “jenseitige(n) Ufer” = the far/opposite bank. “Rath” is the 1857 spelling of Rat (‘counsel, advice’). “übergefahren sein” = to be ferried over (passive of überfahren).

sentences.t: Er gieng weiter und kam zu der Stadt, worin der unfruchtbare Baum stand, und wo der Wächter auch Antwort haben wollte.
sentences.l: He went on and came to the city in which the barren tree stood, and where the watchman too wanted an answer.
sentences.i: He went on and came to the city where the barren tree stood, and where the watchman too wanted his answer.
sentences.n: “unfruchtbar” = barren, unfruitful. “worin” = in which.

sentences.t: Da sagte er ihm, wie er vom Teufel gehört hatte, „tödtet die Maus, die an seiner Wurzel nagt, so wird er wieder goldene Äpfel tragen.“
sentences.l: Then he told him, as he had heard from the Devil, “kill the mouse that gnaws at its root, then it will bear golden apples again.”
sentences.i: Then he told him, as he had heard from the Devil, “kill the mouse that is gnawing at its root, and the tree will bear golden apples again.”
sentences.n: “tödtet” is the 1857 imperative plural of töten (‘kill!’). Note “seiner Wurzel / er” both refer to der Baum (masculine), so ‘its root / it.’

sentences.t: Da dankte ihm der Wächter und gab ihm zur Belohnung zwei mit Gold beladene Esel, die mußten ihm nachfolgen.
sentences.l: Then the watchman thanked him and gave him for reward two donkeys laden with gold, which had to follow after him.
sentences.i: Then the watchman thanked him and gave him, as a reward, two donkeys laden with gold, which had to follow after him.
sentences.n: “beladene” (beladen, ‘to load’) is a participle-adjective, ‘gold-laden.’ “nachfolgen” (‘to follow after’) is separable. So begins the hero’s new wealth — two gold-laden donkeys here, two more at the fountain.

sentences.t: Zuletzt kam er zu der Stadt, deren Brunnen versiegt war.
sentences.l: Last of all he came to the city whose fountain had run dry.
sentences.i: Last of all he came to the city whose fountain had run dry.
sentences.n: “deren” = whose (genitive relative, feminine referring to Stadt). “versiegt” = run dry (see above).

sentences.t: Da sprach er zu dem Wächter, wie der Teufel gesprochen hatte, „es sitzt eine Kröte im Brunnen unter einem Stein, die müßt ihr aufsuchen und tödten, so wird er wieder reichlich Wein geben.“
sentences.l: Then he said to the watchman, as the Devil had spoken, “a toad sits in the fountain under a stone; you must seek it out and kill it, then it will give abundant wine again.”
sentences.i: Then he said to the watchman, as the Devil had said, “a toad sits in the fountain under a stone; you must seek it out and kill it, and the fountain will give wine abundantly again.”
sentences.n: “müßt” is the 1857 spelling of müsst (‘you [pl.] must’). “aufsuchen” (‘to seek out’) is separable. “reichlich” = abundantly. “er” refers to der Brunnen (the fountain).

sentences.t: Der Wächter dankte, und gab ihm ebenfalls zwei mit Gold beladene Esel.
sentences.l: The watchman thanked him, and likewise gave him two donkeys laden with gold.
sentences.i: The watchman thanked him and likewise gave him two donkeys laden with gold.
sentences.n: “ebenfalls” = likewise, also. That makes four gold-laden donkeys in all.

sentences.t: Endlich langte das Glückskind daheim bei seiner Frau an, die sich herzlich freute als sie ihn wiedersah und hörte wie wohl ihm alles gelungen war.
sentences.l: At last the luck-child arrived home at his wife’s, who rejoiced heartily when she saw him again and heard how well everything had succeeded for him.
sentences.i: At last the child of fortune arrived home to his wife, who rejoiced heartily when she saw him again and heard how well everything had gone for him.
sentences.n: “langte ... an” (anlangen, ‘to arrive’) is separable. “wiedersah” (wiedersehen, ‘to see again’) is separable. “gelungen” (gelingen, ‘to succeed’) is impersonal with the dative, ‘it had succeeded for him.’

sentences.t: Dem König brachte er was er verlangt hatte, die drei goldenen Haare des Teufels, und als dieser die vier Esel mit dem Golde sah, ward er ganz vergnügt und sprach „nun sind alle Bedingungen erfüllt und du kannst meine Tochter behalten.
sentences.l: To the king he brought what he had demanded, the three golden hairs of the Devil, and when the latter saw the four donkeys with the gold, he became quite merry and said, “now all the conditions are fulfilled and you can keep my daughter.
sentences.i: To the king he brought what he had asked for, the three golden hairs of the Devil; and when the king saw the four donkeys laden with gold, he grew quite merry and said, “now all the conditions are fulfilled, and you may keep my daughter.
sentences.n: “verlangt” (verlangen, ‘to demand’). “dieser” = ‘the latter,’ i.e. the king. “Golde” has the old dative -e. “ward” again — archaic ‘became.’

sentences.t: Aber, lieber Schwiegersohn, sage mir doch woher ist das viele Gold? das sind ja gewaltige Schätze!“
sentences.l: But, dear son-in-law, do tell me, whence is all this gold? those are mighty treasures indeed!”
sentences.i: But, dear son-in-law, do tell me — where does all this gold come from? These are mighty treasures indeed!”
sentences.n: This is the greedy king’s undoing: his own avarice makes him ask. “gewaltige” = mighty, enormous. “ja” here is an emphatic particle, ‘indeed.’

sentences.t: „Ich bin über einen Fluß gefahren,“ antwortete er, „und da habe ich es mitgenommen, es liegt dort statt des Sandes am Ufer.“
sentences.l: “I crossed over a river,” he answered, “and there I took it along; it lies there instead of sand on the bank.”
sentences.i: “I crossed over a river,” he answered, “and there I took it up; it lies there on the bank in place of sand.”
sentences.n: “Fluß” is the 1857 spelling of Fluss. “mitgenommen” (mitnehmen, ‘to take along’) is separable. “statt des Sandes” = instead of the sand (genitive).

sentences.t: „Kann ich mir auch davon holen?“ sprach der König und war ganz begierig.
sentences.l: “Can I fetch some of it for myself too?” said the king, and was quite eager.
sentences.i: “May I fetch some of it for myself too?” said the king, all eagerness.
sentences.n: “mir ... holen” — the dative mir is for one’s own benefit, ‘fetch for myself.’ “begierig” = eager, greedy — the trait that dooms him.

sentences.t: „So viel ihr nur wollt,“ antwortete er, „es ist ein Fährmann auf dem Fluß, von dem laßt euch überfahren, so könnt ihr drüben eure Säcke füllen.“
sentences.l: “As much as ever you want,” he answered, “there is a ferryman on the river; have yourself ferried across by him, then you can fill your sacks on the other side.”
sentences.i: “As much as ever you please,” he answered; “there is a ferryman on the river — have him ferry you across, and on the far side you can fill your sacks.”
sentences.n: The revenge is set with perfect innocence: ‘let the ferryman carry you over.’ “laßt euch überfahren” = ‘have yourselves ferried across.’ “drüben” = over there, on the other side.

sentences.t: Der habsüchtige König machte sich in aller Eile auf den Weg, und als er zu dem Fluß kam, so winkte er dem Fährmann, der sollte ihn übersetzen.
sentences.l: The avaricious king set off in all haste, and when he came to the river, he beckoned to the ferryman, who was to carry him over.
sentences.i: The greedy king set off in all haste, and when he came to the river, he beckoned to the ferryman to carry him across.
sentences.n: “habsüchtig” = avaricious, grasping. “winkte” (winken, ‘to beckon, wave’). “übersetzen” here = to ferry across (dative der Fährmann).

sentences.t: Der Fährmann kam und hieß ihn einsteigen, und als sie an das jenseitige Ufer kamen, gab er ihm die Ruderstange in die Hand, und sprang davon.
sentences.l: The ferryman came and bade him get in, and when they came to the far bank, he gave him the rowing-pole into his hand, and sprang away.
sentences.i: The ferryman came and bade him step aboard, and when they reached the far bank, he put the rowing-pole into his hand and sprang away.
sentences.n: The curse is passed on exactly as the Devil said. “hieß ... einsteigen” — heißen ‘to bid’ + infinitive; einsteigen (‘to get in, board’) is separable. “Ruderstange” = the rowing-pole (the ferryman’s Stange).

sentences.t: Der König aber mußte von nun an fahren zur Strafe für seine Sünden.
sentences.l: But the king had to ferry from then on, as a punishment for his sins.
sentences.i: But from then on the king had to do the ferrying, as a punishment for his sins.
sentences.n: “von nun an” = from now on. “zur Strafe für” = as a punishment for. “Sünden” is the plural of Sünde (‘sin’). The two death-plots and the greed are all repaid in this single endless sentence of labor.

sentences.t: „Fährt er wohl noch?“
sentences.l: “Is he perhaps still ferrying?”
sentences.i: “Is he ferrying still, do you suppose?”
sentences.n: The tale closes with a little exchange between teller and listener. “wohl” here = ‘I suppose, perhaps.’

sentences.t: „Was denn? es wird ihm niemand die Stange abgenommen haben.“
sentences.l: “What else? no one will have taken the pole from him.”
sentences.i: “What do you think? No one will have taken the pole off his hands.”
sentences.n: “Was denn?” = ‘what else? / what do you think?’ “abgenommen” (abnehmen, ‘to take off, take away’) is separable; “jemandem etwas abnehmen” = to take something off someone’s hands — which no one, of course, will do for the king.
